title: Informed Voting
abstract: Information production by shareholders is essential for proxy voting to produce efficient outcomes. We propose a stock return-based measure to capture informed voting. Our measure, the vote alpha, quantifies the extent to which a shareholder votes in the direction that the market perceives as value increasing. Using data on mutual funds’ proxy voting records, we find that the vote alpha exhibits persistence. Our main result shows that the voting pattern of high vote alpha funds positively predicts long-run abnormal stock returns following contentious votes, suggesting that these funds possess information about the shareholder value implication of contentious governance proposals.

author.Meng Gao: School of Business, University of Connecticut,United States
author.Jiekun Huang*: Gies College of Business, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign,United States

article.1 Measuring Informed Voting:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

A well-functioning shareholder democracy requires that shareholders make informed voting decisions. Since issues that are up for vote at shareholder meetings are often complicated and can have profound impacts on firm value, shareholders must collect information to evaluate how to vote their shares to maximize the value of their holdings. Thus, information production by shareholders is essential for the voting process to lead to improved collective decisions.1Yet, despite the fundamental importance of informed voting for the effectiveness of shareholder voting as a governance mechanism, some important questions remain largely open. In particular, how to measure informed voting? Do informed shareholders’ votes contain information about future stock returns? If so, how does the information content of the votes vary across proposals and firms?

In this paper, we propose a stock return-based approach to measure informed voting and examine the information content of informed shareholders’ votes with respect to future stock returns. We define informed voting as voting in the direction that the market perceives as increasing shareholder value, that is, voting for proposals that the market views as value-enhancing and against proposals that the market views as value-destroying. We use a shareholder’s actual votes and the stock market reaction to the vote outcome to infer whether the shareholder’s votes are informed. The idea is that if an informed shareholder votes for a proposal and the proposal passes or if the shareholder votes against a proposal and the proposal fails, the stock market should react positively to the vote outcome. Conversely, if the informed shareholder votes for a proposal but the proposal fails or if the shareholder votes against a proposal but the proposal passes, the market should react negatively. This predicts that a mimicking portfolio that goes long stocks at which an informed shareholder wins the vote (ie, the vote outcome goes in the direction of the shareholder’s vote) and goes short stocks at which the same shareholder loses the vote (ie, the vote outcome goes in the opposite direction of the shareholder’s vote) should deliver positive abnormal returns. The alpha on the mimicking portfolio captures the extent to which a shareholder votes in the direction that increases stock prices. This is analogous to the tests for informed trading where trade informativeness is captured by the extent to which investors trade in the direction of future stock price movements.

We examine informed voting using data on mutual funds’ proxy votes. Mutual funds provide an ideal setting to examine informed voting for three reasons. First, according to Federal Reserve Board Flow of Funds data, mutual funds collectively own about 21.7% of U.S. corporate equities as of the end of 2019, making them an important group of shareholders in corporate proxy voting. Second, mutual funds have to disclose their votes, which allows us to infer the extent to which they vote in the direction that the market perceives as value enhancing. Third, compared to individual investors, mutual funds have the scale and resources to engage in information production. Importantly, mutual funds have a fiduciary responsibility to cast votes in the best interests of their shareholders. As SEC Acting Chair Allison H.Lee (2021)put it, “the act of voting is itself a critical part of funds’ and advisers’ fiduciary obligations.” To fulfill this responsibility, mutual fund managers may opt to conduct costly information production to evaluate the proposals up for vote.

Using a large sample of proxy votes by mutual funds on contentious governance proposals from 2003 to 2018, we construct a measure of informed voting for individual funds. The measure, vote alpha, captures the abnormal stock return around shareholder meetings when a fund wins a contentious vote relative to when the fund loses, weighted by the fund’s holdings in the stocks. A more positive value of the measure indicates that a fund is better informed about the market’s perception of the value implication of the proposals up for vote. We find that the vote alpha has a mean close to zero for our sample of fund-years, suggesting that on average mutual funds do not vote in a way aligned with the market’s perception. The vote alpha displays considerable variation across funds. We thus sort our sample mutual funds into quintiles based on the vote alpha in each year and classify those in the top quintile as high vote alpha funds.

Consistent with the vote alpha capturing a somewhat persistent fund characteristic, the 1-year lagged value of the vote alpha positively and significantly predicts the current vote alpha. For example, other things equal, the likelihood of a fund being a high vote alpha fund in a year is about 3.2 percentage points higher if the fund was a high vote alpha fund in the previous year, representing 16.0% of the unconditional probability. The vote alpha exhibits significant heterogeneity in the cross-section of mutual funds. In particular, funds with a lower turnover rate are associated with a higher vote alpha, suggesting that long-term investors are more likely to produce information in the voting process. Also, the tendency to follow ISS recommendations is a negative and significant predictor of the vote alpha, suggesting that active funds, that is, those that rely less on ISS’s recommendations, are informed. Further, the tendency to vote against management negatively predicts the vote alpha, but this relation is driven entirely by the tendency to follow ISS’s recommendations to vote against management, rather than by the tendency to vote against management when ISS recommends otherwise.

We then examine the predictive power of informed voting for abnormal stock returns following contentious votes. Informed shareholders’ votes in aggregate may contain information that has not been reflected in stock prices. When informed voters disproportionately vote for a contentious proposal, they likely possess favorable information about the proposal. Thus, the stock price should increase (decrease) following the passage (rejection) of the proposal to reflect the information possessed by informed voters. Conversely, when informed voters disproportionately vote against a contentious proposal, the stock price should increase (decrease) following the rejection (passage) of the proposal. This suggests that abnormal stock returns following a contentious vote should increase with the extent to which the vote outcome goes in the direction favored by informed voters, which we measure by the fractional ownership of informed voters that win the vote relative to those that lose the vote (hereafter referred to as net win of informed voters).

Consistent with the idea that high vote alpha funds’ votes contain information about future stock returns, we find that net win of high vote alpha funds positively predicts abnormal stock returns at horizons of up to 18 months after the vote on contentious proposals. The economic magnitude is large. For example, a one-standard-deviation increase in net win of high vote alpha funds is associated with an increase of 1.239 percentage points in the Fama-French-Carhart four-factor adjusted cumulative abnormal return (CAR) over a 6-month window after the vote, and the magnitude is roughly the same for longer holding horizons. In contrast, net win of other funds does not possess predictive power for abnormal stock returns following the votes. The difference in the predictive power for abnormal stock returns between net win of high vote alpha funds and that of other funds is statistically significant at conventional levels when we look at stock returns over 3-, 6-, and 12-month horizons. These results suggest that high vote alpha funds possess information about the shareholder value implication of contentious governance proposals and that such information is impounded into the stock price over time after the vote.

The marginal value of information produced by mutual funds might vary in the cross-section of proposals. We find that the predictive power of net win of high vote alpha funds for subsequent abnormal stock returns is stronger for proposals on which proxy advisors tend to issue blanket recommendations, suggesting that information produced by high vote alpha funds is more valuable when proxy advisors provide less informative recommendations. We also find evidence that the value of information about a proposal increases with the ex ante likelihood that the proposal will be contentious. Further, consistent with poor past performance and high retail ownership increasing the value of information, the predictive power is stronger among poorly performing firms and firms with high retail ownership.

If high vote alpha funds produce information on governance issues, they might play an effective monitoring role and lead to improved firm performance. This predicts that, other things equal, a higher level of ownership by high vote alpha funds should be positively correlated with subsequent firm performance. Consistent with this prediction, we find that informed ownership, defined as the proportion of mutual fund ownership accounted for by high vote alpha funds, positively predicts return on assets (ROA) and standardized unexpected earnings based on analyst forecasts (SUE). Using a calendar-time portfolio approach, we also find that informed ownership positively predicts subsequent stock returns, especially those around shareholder meetings. These results are consistent with high vote alpha funds playing a monitoring role that enhances shareholder value.

Our paper makes four contributions to the literature. First, we propose a novel approach to infer information production by investors in proxy voting. Our paper is the first to use stock returns around shareholder meetings and a shareholder’s votes to infer the extent to which the shareholder casts informed votes. By relying on the stock market’s reaction to vote outcomes, our approach yields a direct, albeit potentially noisy, measure of the extent to which a fund casts informed votes.2Our paper thus complements existing studies on institutional investors’ proxy voting behavior that focus onactivevoting behavior, such as voting against management’s or proxy advisors’ recommendations.3,4Our results indicate that announcement returns convey valuable information about the market’s perception of proposal quality, which is noteworthy in light of recent work byLevit, Malenko, and Maug (2024)that raises concerns about the potential misalignment between price reactions and shareholder welfare.

Second, our paper is the first to show evidence that informed voters possess information about future stock returns. Our finding that net win of high vote alpha funds predicts future stock returns highlights the value of information in the voting process. Since vote outcomes can directly affect corporate policies, our results suggest that increased information production in proxy voting can lead to more efficient real decisions. In this respect, our paper contributes to the literature on the effect of shareholder voting on corporate policies and shareholder value (see, among others,Cai, Garner, and Walkling 2009;Cuñat, Giné, and Guadalupe 2012,2016;Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2013,2015;Fos and Tsoutsoura 2014;Larcker, McCall, and Ormazabal 2015;Malenko and Shen 2016;Fos 2017;Fos, Li, and Tsoutsoura 2014;Holderness 2018;Aggarwal, Dahiya, and Prabhala 2019;Brav et al. 2024).

Third, our paper contributes to the literature on the role of mutual funds, and institutional investors more generally, as information producers in financial markets. Prior studies have examined the information production role of institutional investors through their trades (e.g.,Chen, Jegadeesh, and Wermers 2000;Sias, Starks, and Titman 2006;Chemmanur, Hu, and Huang 2010,2015). Our paper sheds new light on this role by looking at their proxy votes. Like stock trading, proxy voting is an important mechanism to aggregate diverse information of investors.5Methodologically, our tests linking shareholders’ net win to post-vote abnormal stock returns offer a new approach to extracting information held by shareholders in voting.

Last, our analysis indicates substantial heterogeneity in informed voting across mutual funds and across proposals. In particular, funds with a long-term focus and those that rely less on proxy advisors’ recommendations tend to cast informed votes. In the cross-section of proposals, the marginal value of information produced by mutual funds is higher when proxy advisors produce less precise information, when the proposal up for vote has a higher ex ante likelihood of being contentious, and when the firm has worse past performance or more retail ownership. These findings shed light on the forces shaping shareholders’ incentive to produce information on governance issues (e.g.,Kahn and Winton 1998;Malenko and Malenko 2019).

Our study has important implications for regulators. Informed voting is a public good, and as such, policies should be put in place to promote and facilitate it. Our paper provides a crucial step forward by proposing a measure of informed voting, which can be used to evaluate the effectiveness of such policies. Moreover, our findings on the stock return predictive power of high vote alpha funds’ votes suggest that increased information production in proxy voting can lead to more efficient outcomes. These results contribute to a better understanding of the importance of informed voting and can inform policymakers in formulating effective policies to enhance information production in proxy voting.
We use a simple conceptual framework to develop our measure of informed voting. The premise is that fund managers vote based on their information about the valuation impact of a contentious proposal.6That is, fund managers vote for (against) a proposal if they possess information that the passage of the proposal would increase (decrease) firm value.

Suppose the value of a contentious proposal depends on whether the true state,τ, isGorB, which is unknown to investors. If the true state isG, a contentious proposal would change firm value byv(v> 0) if passed and would have no effect on firm value if failed. Conversely, if the true state isB, a contentious proposal would change firm value by—vif passed and would have no effect on firm value if failed.7Letθ∈(0,1)denote the common prior probability that the true state isG, andp∈(0,1)denote the common prior probability that the proposal passes. The parameters,θ,p, andvare common knowledge. The pre-vote market price reflects the market expectation about the true state and the likelihood of passage. As such, if the state isG, the stock market reaction to the passage of the proposal isv−(2θ−1)pvand that to the rejection is−(2θ−1)pv⁠. Similarly, if the state isB, the stock market reaction to the passage of the proposal is−v−(2θ−1)pvand that to the rejection is−(2θ−1)pv⁠. Before the vote on the proposal, fundfacquires a private signal,ξf, about the true state of the world. The signal is eitherGorBwithPr(ξf=G|τ=G)=Pr(ξf=B|τ=B)=πf⁠. Thus,πfcaptures the extent to which fundfis informed about the unknown state and hence the value of the proposal.

Similarly, when fund f votes against a proposal (ie, when the private signal is B ), the fund wins the vote if the proposal fails. To operationalize this framework, we use the stock returns around contentious votes to measure the change in firm value and consider a portfolio of contentious proposals. We compute the vote alpha as the alpha on a mimicking portfolio that goes long stocks at which a fund wins the vote and goes short stocks at which the fund loses the vote. Formally, for a fund f that votes on P contentious proposals in a given year, the vote alpha of the fund-year is defined as follows, Vote α f = ∑ p = 1 P C A R p × I p , f × v p , f ∑ p = 1 P v p , f , (4) where CAR p is the cumulative market-adjusted return around the vote on proposal p ; I p , f is a win/lose indicator, which equals 1 if fund f wins the vote and –1 if fund f loses; v p , f is the dollar value of fund f ’s holdings in the stock at which proposal p is held before the vote. 8 We use the value of holdings as weights, because fund managers are likely to allocate more research effort to governance issues at firms where they hold a larger stake. The vote alpha is essentially a weighted average CAR associated with winning relative to losing votes. A more positive value of the vote alpha indicates that the mutual fund is better informed about the market’s perception of the shareholder value implications of the proposals.

We focus on contentious votes to construct the vote alpha for two reasons. First, since the outcome of contentious votes is generally unanticipated, the stock returns around such votes provide a reasonably clean measure of the market’s perception of the impact of the vote outcome on stock value. In contrast, the outcome of noncontentious votes is generally well anticipated, and accordingly the stock returns are unlikely to convey much information about noncontentious votes. Second, contentious proposals, compared to noncontentious ones, are likely to require a greater amount of information production by shareholders. In contrast, some noncontentious proposals (e.g., ratifying an auditor) may have limited shareholder value implications and therefore may not require much information production by shareholders.9Thus, mutual funds’ votes on contentious proposals can provide useful information about the extent to which they make informed voting decisions.

A few comments are in order regarding our measure. First, while the vote alpha measure provides valuable insights into a fund’s informed voting behavior, it is important to acknowledge that it is potentially noisy. The stock return around the vote on a contentious proposal may reflect factors other than the direct valuation impact of the proposal. To the extent that such factors are not systematically correlated with the valuation impact, they are likely to introduce noise into our vote alpha measure and lead to attenuation bias in our results.10

Second, since the vote alpha is based on the stock market reaction around the shareholder meeting where a contentious vote is held, it may underestimate the informativeness of a fund’s votes if the fund discloses its votes before the shareholder meeting and can sway the vote outcome. In this case, the stock market would react to the information possessed by the fund before the shareholder meeting, that is, when the fund’s intended votes are made public.11In practice, however, only a small number of mutual funds, most of which have a socially responsible investing focus, preannounce their proxy votes, suggesting that the vote alpha is likely to be a valid measure of informed voting for a large majority of funds in our sample.

Third, the premise of our measure is that fund managers vote based on their information about the valuation impact of a proposal. Our measure does not capture the informativeness of a fund’s votes if strategic or portfolio considerations influence voting decisions (e.g.,Maug 1999;Matvos and Ostrovsky 2008;Maug and Rydqvist 2009;He, Huang, and Zhao 2019;Bar-Isaac and Shapiro 2020; Antón et al. 2002). Also, if a fund’s voting decisions are driven by nonpecuniary factors, such as social and environmental impacts, the fund is likely to be considered as uninformed under our measure to the extent that the nonpecuniary factors are uncorrelated with the valuation impact. However, because we restrict our analysis to governance proposals (as opposed to social and environmental ones), the concern about nonpecuniary considerations in voting decisions is unlikely to be important in our setting.

Last, the vote alpha measure differs from active voting measures in the literature (e.g.,Iliev and Lowry 2015;Gantchev and Giannetti 2021) in that our measure relies on stock market outcomes, whereas the active voting measures rely on deviations from “passive” voting behavior. Just as trading in the direction of future stock price movements indicates informed trading, voting in the direction of stock market reactions indicates informed voting. In this sense, our measure provides a straightforward and direct estimate of the extent to which a fund casts informed votes.

With the vote alpha defined at the fund level, we now turn to the construction of net win measures at the proposal level. We partition mutual funds into informed and uninformed groups and construct net win measures separately for each group. Specifically, at the end of the month before a contentious proposal is put to a vote, we compute the vote alpha for each fund using its votes during the most recent 12 months. We then sort mutual funds into quintiles by the vote alpha and classify those in the top quintile as high vote alpha funds, which are likely informed voters. To extract the information possessed by high vote alpha funds about the proposal, we defineNetWin(Informed) as the difference between the fractional ownership of high vote alpha funds that win the vote and that of high vote alpha funds that lose the vote. While the vote alpha for individual funds can be noisy, by aggregating the votes across a significant number of funds that win or lose a contentious vote and taking the difference, the net win measure at the proposal level is likely less susceptible to the influence of noise. We similarly defineNetWin(Uninformed) as net win of funds not in the top quintile of the vote alpha. These measures essentially capture the extent to which the vote outcome goes in the direction favored by each of the two groups of funds.

A more positive value ofNetWin(Informed) indicates that a contentious proposal passes (fails) when high vote alpha funds disproportionately vote for (against) the proposal. If high vote alpha funds possess information about the proposal up for vote that has not been incorporated into stock prices,NetWin(Informed) should positively predict future abnormal stock returns. In contrast,NetWin(Uninformed) should not have such predictive power.
article.2 Data and Sample:
2.1 Data on mutual funds’ votes

We focus on mutual funds’ votes on governance proposals, defined as those relating to corporate governance practices such as antitakeover defenses, shareholder rights, executive compensation, voting, and board structure (see, e.g.,Gillan and Starks 2000;Cuñat, Giné, and Guadalupe 2012). We list the proposal types in our sample inInternet Appendix Table IA-1. We focus on governance proposals for two reasons. First, the outcome of contentious votes on governance proposals can greatly affect the stock value (e.g.,Cai and Walkling 2011;Cuñat, Giné, and Guadalupe 2012,2016;Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2015;Gantchev and Giannetti 2021;Babenko, Choi, and Sen 2022). Second, making voting decisions on governance proposals often requires information production, because a governance arrangement that maximizes shareholder value for one firm may not maximize it for another. Since there is no one-size-fits-all approach to corporate governance, information production by shareholders is essential for the voting process to lead to efficient outcomes.

We obtain data on mutual funds’ votes on governance proposals during the period from July 2003 through June 2018 from the Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) Voting Analytics database. The detailed voting information becomes available following the Securities and Exchange Commission’s Rule 30b1-4, which requires all mutual funds registered in the United States to report their proxy votes in all shareholder meetings of their portfolio companies using Form N-PX starting from 2003. For each proposed agenda item (ie, proposal) voted on by each mutual fund, the data report the firm that receives the proposal, the date of the shareholder meeting during which the proposal is considered, the issue being voted on, the sponsor of the proposal, management’s recommendation, ISS’s recommendation, and the fund’s vote. We obtain data on vote outcomes from the ISS Voting Results data set.

We merge the voting data with CRSP Survivor-Bias-Free U.S. Mutual Fund Database. The CRSP database provides detailed information on fund characteristics, such as size, turnover, loads, expenses, and returns. Because there is no common identifier for mutual funds across the two databases, we use a name-matching procedure to match funds in the two data sets. We are able to identify 11, 976 funds in the voting database that are in the CRSP database. These mutual funds cast about 87.4% of the votes covered by the voting database during the sample period. We then link a fund’s vote on a proposal at a firm to the fund’s holdings in the firm’s stock using the most recent quarterly holdings report before the vote. We retrieve holdings from Thomson Reuters Mutual Fund Holdings Database and complement the data with holdings from the CRSP Mutual Fund database.

Panel A ofTable 1reports summary statistics on mutual funds’ votes on governance proposals. As described above, computing a mutual fund’s vote alpha in a year requires a portfolio of votes on contentious governance proposals. We define contentious governance proposals as those that pass or fail within±20%around the passing threshold. A±20%cutoff allows us to have a reasonably large sample of funds with a sufficient number of contentious votes. Panel A shows that votes on contentious governance proposals account for about 10.8% of all mutual fund votes. We defineWin voteas an indicator that equals one if a fund’s vote is in the direction of the vote outcome (e.g., a fund votes for a proposal and the proposal passes). Consistent with the contested nature of contentious proposals, the averageWin votefor mutual funds’ votes on contentious proposals is 55.8%. Thus, it is roughly equally likely for a fund to win or lose a vote on contentious proposals. In contrast, the averageWin votefor the sample of noncontentious proposals is 94.7%, suggesting that there is little uncertainty about the outcome of noncontentious proposals. Also, 51.6% of the votes on contentious proposals go against management recommendations and 63.7% follow ISS recommendations, as compared to 6.3% and 94.7%, respectively, for votes on noncontentious proposals. Therefore, while votes on contentious proposals represent a relatively small fraction of the votes by mutual funds, such proposals are likely to account for a disproportionate amount of information production.

Panel B ofTable 1lists the top 10 contentious governance proposal types ranked by the number of votes. The most common types are advisory votes to ratify named executive officers’ compensation (representing 17.8% of the votes on contentious proposals), followed by proposals to enhance shareholders’ ability to call a special meeting (8.4%), and to require an independent board chairperson (8.3%). Taken together, votes on the top 10 contentious governance proposal types account for 69.8% of the votes on all contentious governance proposals.

2.2 Summary statistics on the sample of mutual funds

We first construct a sample of mutual funds to examine persistence in the vote alpha and its cross-sectional determinants. We conduct our analysis at the fund level rather than the fund family level, because mutual funds appear to exercise considerable independence in their votes on contentious proposals. For a given contentious proposal, the likelihood that two funds vote differently on the proposal is 34.6% when the two funds are from the same family. To put this number in perspective, the likelihood is 47.7% when the two funds are from different families. Thus, within-family differences in votes on contentious proposals are large and comparable to across-family differences, suggesting that analysis at the fund level is appropriate.12

To be included in our sample, we require that the fund be classified by CRSP as a domestic equity fund, have a TNA of at least$5million, and cast votes on more than 10 contentious governance proposals in the year. The sample consists of 13, 504 fund-years.

We compute the vote alpha for each fund-year in our sample using the market’s reaction to vote outcomes over a 12-day window around the vote (from day –1 to + 10, with day 0 being the date of the vote). We use a 12-day window to accommodate the possibility that it might take some time for the market to fully incorporate the value impact of contentious proposals. This is especially relevant if the close vote is due to uncertainty about the proposal’s value impact, as the market may need time to assess its implications.13

Panel A ofTable 2shows that the vote alpha has a mean close to zero at –0.042%, suggesting that, conditional on information publicly available at the time of the vote, the average mutual fund’s votes do not contain new information. Since information production is privately costly but the gain from the vote outcome going in a certain direction accrues to all shareholders, this result is consistent with the average fund manager having limited incentives to produce information on governance issues. Moreover, fund managers’ information production incentives could be undermined by conflicts of interest (e.g.,Davis and Kim 2007;Butler and Gurun 2012), thereby leading to uninformed votes.

Importantly, the vote alpha exhibits considerable cross-sectional variation across funds with a standard deviation of 1.452 percentage points. We thus sort our sample mutual funds into quintiles based on the vote alpha in each year and classify those in the top quintile as high vote alpha funds, which are likely informed voters.Internet Appendix Table IA-2lists the top 10 funds and fund families in terms of the vote alpha.

Panel A ofTable 2also shows that the average sample fund has a TNA of about$3.2billion, belongs to a fund family with a TNA of$332.8billion, has been in existence for about 188 months, and has 239 stocks in its portfolio. These numbers are much larger than those for the average domestic equity fund in the CRSP database during the same period (e.g., the latter has a TNA of about$1.3billion and belongs to a family with a TNA of$187.4billion). Thus, our sample is tilted toward larger funds, which is expected given that we require a fund to vote on more than 10 contentious governance proposals in a given year.14On average, funds in our sample have an annual turnover ratio of 73.9%, an expense ratio of 0.9%, and a total load of 1.3% of TNA. FollowingKacperczyk, Sialm, and Zheng (2005), we calculate the value-weighted size, value, and momentum scores for each fund-year in our sample. The scores range from 1 to 5. The average fund has a size score of 4.3, a value score of 2.3, and a momentum score of 3.5, suggesting that the average fund tilts its portfolio heavily toward large-cap stocks and slightly toward growth stocks and winner stocks. The tilt toward large-cap stocks is expected, because large-cap companies tend to attract more shareholder proposals, which are more likely to have a contentious vote outcome than management proposals. SeeTable A.1 in the appendixfor definitions of all variables.

We also construct three measures to capture mutual funds’ voting behavior on governance proposals.%vote with ISSis the fraction of a fund’s votes in a year that are in line with ISS recommendations.%vote against managementis the fraction of a fund’s votes in a year that go against management’s recommendations.%sole/deviating vote from familyis the fraction of a fund’s votes that represent either a sole vote or a deviating vote from other funds in the same family on a given proposal. We define a vote cast by a fund on a proposal as a sole vote if the fund is the only fund in its family that votes on the proposal. We define a vote as a deviating vote when at least two same-family funds cast votes on the proposal, and the fund’s vote differs from the vote of the majority of same-family funds.15Panel A ofTable 2shows that, for the average fund-year in our sample, about 89.6%, 13.1%, and 19.0% of the votes on governance proposals are in line with ISS’s recommendations, against management’s recommendations, and represent either a sole vote or a deviating vote from other same-family funds, respectively.

2.3 Summary statistics on the sample of contentious proposals

To examine the valuation impact of informed voting, we use a sample of 7, 422 contentious governance proposals voted on by at least one mutual fund in our sample. We use ex ante information to construct the net win measures for each proposal. As described in Section 1, at the end of the month before the vote on a contentious proposal, we compute the vote alpha for each fund using its votes during the most recent 12 months. We then sort mutual funds into quintiles by the vote alpha and classify those in the top quintile as high vote alpha funds. We computeNetWin(Informed)by taking the difference between the aggregate fractional ownership of high vote alpha funds that win the vote and that of high vote alpha funds that lose the vote. Panel B ofTable 2shows thatNetWin(Informed)has a mean of 0.615 percentage points and a standard deviation of 2.474 percentage points. The median ofNetWin(Informed)is close to zero at 0.052 percentage points, and about 54.9% of the sample have a positive value ofNetWin(Informed)⁠. This suggests that, on net, high vote alpha funds as a whole are roughly equally likely to win or lose the vote on a contentious proposal. We similarly computeNetWin(Uninformed)using the fractional ownership of funds not in the top quintile of the vote alpha.NetWin(Uninformed)has a mean of 3.078 percentage points and a standard deviation of 6.470 percentage points. BothNetWin(Informed)andNetWin(Uninformed)are positively skewed, which is expected because when a number of shareholders with significant holdings vote in the same direction on a proposal, they are likely to sway the outcome of the vote in their favor.

We compute cumulative abnormal returns following the shareholder meetings at which contentious proposals are voted on using the Fama-French-Carhart four-factor model and DGTW characteristics benchmarks. We consider four relatively long holding horizons after the vote on contentious proposals, that is, 3 months (ie, day + 1 to + 63, with day 0 being the date of the vote), 6 months (+ 1 to + 126), 12 months (+ 1 to + 252), and 18 months (+ 1 to + 378). The use of relatively long holding horizons allows us to focus on the permanent change in stock prices due to information production and minimize the noise introduced by noninformational factors, such as temporary price pressure and liquidity effects. Another reason for using relatively long-horizon returns is that it may take time for the market to react to the information contained inNetWin(Informed)⁠. Because mutual funds’ votes and holdings are disclosed with a lag,16investors do not know in real time which funds are likely informed or how informed funds have voted on a given proposal. Thus, the market is unlikely to impound the information possessed by informed funds within a short period of time.

Panel B ofTable 2shows summary statistics on the stock return measures. For example, the 6-month Fama-French-Carhart four-factor adjusted (DGTW characteristics-adjusted) CARs have a mean of –0.408% (–0.518%) and a standard deviation of 31.997 (28.168) percentage points. The panel also reports summary statistics of various proposal and firm characteristics.Management winsis an indicator that equals one if the outcome of the vote goes in the same direction as management’s recommendation and zero otherwise. Similarly,ISS winsis an indicator that equals one if the outcome of the vote goes in the same direction as ISS’s recommendation and zero otherwise. Management and ISS win 80.7% and 40.5% of the contentious votes, respectively.Management loses noncontentious is an indicator that equals one if management loses noncontentious votes (ie, losing margin greater than 20%) at the shareholder meeting where the contentious proposal is voted on. About 3.3% of contentious proposals in our sample are accompanied by management losing noncontentious votes. We also construct control variables, including total assets, market-to-book ratio, prior stock return, leverage, capital expenditures, S&P 500 membership, mutual fund ownership, and analyst coverage. All of the control variables are measured using the most recent information publicly available to investors at the time when a given proposal is voted on.

2.4 Summary statistics on the sample of firms

We construct a sample of firm-years to examine the performance implications of ownership by high vote alpha funds. The sample includes all common stocks traded on the NYSE, Nasdaq, and AMEX with nonmissing data. To capture the influence of informed voters, we defineInformed ownershipas the fraction of a firm’s mutual fund ownership accounted for by high vote alpha funds. The assumption underlying this measure is that the distribution of informed voters is similar across mutual funds and non-mutual-fund investors. Panel C ofTable 2shows thatInformed ownershiphas a mean of 18.1% and a standard deviation of 0.166.

We use two measures of operating performance. The first is return on assets (ROA), and the second is the standardized unexpected earnings (SUE). We calculate the SUE as the difference between reported annual earnings per share (EPS) and the median of the most recent EPS forecasts of all analysts issued over the 1-year period prior to the earnings announcement, scaled by the stock price. ROA has a mean of –0.035 and a standard deviation of 0.283, and the SUE has a mean of –0.0054 and a standard deviation of 0.068.

For each firm-year, we compute the fraction of governance proposals for which the vote outcome is in the direction of management’s recommendation (% Management wins) as well as that in the direction of ISS’s recommendation (% ISS wins). Management and ISS on average win the vote on 98.2% and 91.3% of the proposals, respectively. These numbers are considerably higher than those for contentious proposals, because a large majority of the proposals are noncontentious. Panel C ofTable 2also reports summary statistics of the same set of firm characteristics as in panel B. The two panels show that firms with contentious proposals tend to be larger, more likely to be in the S&P 500 index, and have a higher market-to-book ratio, than the average firm in the sample of firm-years.
article.3 Empirical Tests:
In this section, we first examine the persistence and determinants of the vote alpha among mutual funds. We then examine the information content of the voting pattern of high vote alpha funds using the sample of contentious proposals. Last, we examine the relation between informed ownership and subsequent operating and stock performance.

3.1 Persistence and determinants of the vote alpha

We analyze the persistence and determinants of the vote alpha using a panel regression. Specifically, we run the following regression using the sample of fund-years: Y f , t = α + β × Y f , t − 1 + γ × X f , t − 1 + ε f , t , (5) where Y f , t is either the vote alpha or the high vote alpha indicator for fund f in year t ; X f , t − 1 consists of a comprehensive set of fund characteristics measured in year t— 1, including fund size, fund family size, fund age, an indicator for index funds, past style-adjusted fund return and return volatility, past style-adjusted fund flow and flow volatility, number of stocks, turnover rate, expense ratio, load, size score, value score, momentum score, the tendency to vote with ISS, the tendency to vote against management, and the tendency to cast a sole or deviating vote from other funds in the same family. We additionally control for style fixed effects and year fixed effects in all regressions. We cluster standard errors at the fund family level to allow for arbitrary within-family correlation in residuals.

The results, reported inTable 3, show that the vote alpha exhibits a moderate level of persistence.17A fund with a high vote alpha in the previous year is likely to have a high vote alpha in the current year. In terms of economic magnitudes, column 5 shows that after controlling for observable fund characteristics, the likelihood that a fund ranks in the top quintile of the vote alpha in a year is about 3.2 percentage points higher when it ranks in the top quintile in the previous year, which represents about 16.0% of the unconditional probability. This result suggests that the vote alpha likely captures a somewhat stable characteristic of mutual funds’ voting behavior. We note, however, that the observed persistence may be subject to attenuation bias due to noise in the vote alpha measure, as discussed above.18

In terms of fund characteristics, the turnover rate significantly negatively predicts the vote alpha. For example, columns 5 and 6 show that an increase from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile in the turnover rate is associated with a decrease of roughly 1.2 and 1.4 percentage points, respectively, in the likelihood of being a high vote alpha fund. This represents a 6.0% to 7% decrease relative to the unconditional probability. This result suggests that long-term investors are more likely to produce information in the voting process, which is consistent with the view that such investors play a monitoring role (e.g.,Chen, Harford, and Li 2007;McCahery, Sautner, and Starks 2016). Since the payoff from improved corporate governance is inherently long-term, shareholders taking a long-term perspective have a stronger incentive to conduct proxy research and are more likely to reap the benefit from their research.

Moreover, the tendency to vote with ISS’s recommendations is a negative and significant predictor of the vote alpha. This result provides evidence that active voters, that is, those that rely less on ISS’s recommendations, are informed. In terms of economic magnitudes, columns 5 and 6 show that a one-standard-deviation increase in the tendency to vote with ISS is associated with a decrease of roughly 1.3 percentage points in the likelihood of being a high vote alpha fund (or a 6.5% decrease relative to the unconditional probability).

Similarly, the tendency to vote against management’s recommendations is a negative and significant predictor of the vote alpha. However, this negative relation is mainly driven by the tendency to vote against management following ISS’s recommendations. To explore this, we decompose the tendency to vote against management into two parts: voting against management following ISS’s against recommendations (ISS-conforming against votes) and voting against management when ISS recommends voting for management (non-conforming against votes). We then replace the tendency to vote against management with these two parts and repeat the regressions.Table IA-3 in the Internet Appendixshows that the coefficient for the tendency to cast ISS-conforming against votes is negative and significant at the 1% level, whereas that on the tendency to cast non-conforming against votes is insignificant. Thus, it is not that voting against management in general is indicative of uninformed voting; rather, uninformed funds tend to indiscriminately follow ISS’s recommendations to vote against management. Interestingly, the coefficient for the tendency to vote with ISS’s recommendations becomes insignificant inTable IA-3. Taken together, these results suggest that funds that conduct independent research when ISS recommends voting against management (as opposed to when ISS recommends voting for management) are particularly well informed.

Furthermore, some evidence indicates that funds with fewer stocks in their portfolios, funds that tilt their portfolios toward small stocks and growth stocks, and funds with a greater tendency to cast a sole or deviating vote from other funds in the same family are associated a higher likelihood of being in the top quintile of vote alpha,19although these characteristics are insignificant in predicting the level of vote alpha. We also find that fund size and family size are insignificant in predicting the vote alpha. Since the funds in our sample are relatively large and belong to large families, we caution against overinterpreting these results.

The overall fit of these regressions is low as measured by the adjustedR2, indicating a considerable amount of unexplained variability in the vote alpha. We note, however, that regressions with stock return-based measures of mutual fund skills as the dependent variable tend to have lowR2values because of the large variability in stock returns (see, e.g.,table 7ofKacperczyk, Sialm, and Zheng 2008;table 6ofvan Binsbergen et al. 2024).

To assess the robustness of the persistence of high vote alpha, we use four alternative cutoffs, that is, the top ventile, top decile, top quartile, and top tercile, in addition to the top quintile to define high vote alpha funds. We regress an indicator for high vote alpha funds using each of these cutoffs on its lagged value and fund characteristics.20Panel A ofTable 4shows that the coefficient on the lagged high vote alpha indicator is positive and significant across all cutoffs. The bootstrappedp-value for the coefficient is also significant at conventional levels in all specifications, alleviating concerns about the persistence of high vote alpha being driven by sampling variability. We perform similar tests to examine the persistence of low vote alpha. Panel B shows that the coefficient on the lagged indicator for low vote alpha funds is insignificant and generally close to zero across all cutoffs. A Wald test rejects the equality of the coefficients on the lagged dependent variables in the same column between the two panels at the 1% level. Thus, high vote alpha is significantly more likely to be persistent than low vote alpha, suggesting that it is more likely driven by stable fund traits.

To show the correlation between vote alpha and its lagged value in the raw data, we group funds into lagged vote alpha bins with a width of half a percentage point.Figure 1shows that the vote alpha is generally an increasing function of its lagged value and that funds with a high vote alpha are more likely to exhibit persistence than those with a low one. In fact, the relation between the vote alpha and its lagged value is essentially flat when the lagged value is low. These results, along with those inTable 4, suggest that fund managers with a high vote alpha may have superior skills in proxy research, which enable them to consistently vote in the direction that increases shareholder value. Conversely, fund managers with a low vote alpha may lack such skills and do not consistently vote in the direction that increases or decreases shareholder value.

Fig. 1 Vote alpha as a function of its lagged value This figure shows the relation between the vote alpha and its 1-year-lagged value. We group funds into lagged vote alpha bins with a width of half a percentage point. The top panel plots the mean vote alpha for each bin and the fitted line. The bottom panel performs a kernel-weighted local polynomial regression of vote alpha on the lagged vote alpha bins and plots the smoothed values with 95% confidence bands. We use the Stata function lpoly to estimate the kernel regressions, with the default options of local mean smoothing and the Epanechnikov kernel. A high vote alpha may arise when a fund conducts more intensive proxy research. To shed light on this, we use EDGAR log files to infer fund companies’ proxy research effort (followingIliev, Kalodimos, and Lowry 2021).21We construct two measures of proxy research at the fund company-year level. The first is the number of distinct IP addresses associated with a fund company that view proxy statements in a year. To the extent that computers at fund companies use fixed IP addresses, this measure is likely to capture the number of people working on proxy research at the fund company. The second measure is the total number of views of proxy statements by a fund company in a year. For both measures, we focus on viewing of a firm’s proxy statements during a 75-day period immediately before the firm’s shareholder meeting (followingIliev, Kalodimos, and Lowry 2021).

We regress vote alpha at the fund company-year level on lagged measures of proxy research. The vote alpha for a fund company in a year is computed as the weighted average of affiliated funds’ vote alpha in that year, using total net assets as weights. The first two columns ofInternet Appendix Table IA-4show that the two measures are positive and significant predictors of the vote alpha. In terms of economic significance, an interquartile (interdecile) increase in the number of distinct IP addresses with proxy views is associated with an increase of 0.051 (0.190) percentage points in the vote alpha, which is nontrivial considering that the vote alpha for fund companies has a standard deviation of 1.224 percentage points. We also scale the number of distinct IP addresses and the number of views by the number of shareholder meetings at which the fund company casts votes. The last four columns show that the coefficients on the scaled measures and indicators for these scaled measures being in the top quintile are positive and significant. These results provide suggestive evidence that proxy research is a channel through which mutual funds make informed voting decisions, increasing confidence in the validity of our vote alpha measure in capturing informed voting.22

3.2 The information content of shareholders’ votes

If high vote alpha funds are informed about the shareholder value implications of a contentious proposal up for vote, their aggregate votes may contain information that has not been reflected in stock prices. Specifically, when high vote alpha funds disproportionately vote for a contentious proposal, they likely possess favorable information about the proposal. As such, the stock price should increase following the passage and decrease following the rejection of the proposal to reflect the information. Conversely, when high vote alpha funds disproportionately vote against a contentious proposal, they likely possess unfavorable information about the proposal. In this case, the stock price should increase following the rejection and decrease following the passage of the proposal. Hence, abnormal stock returns following the vote should increase with the extent to which the vote outcome goes in the direction favored by high vote alpha funds, which is measured by net win of these funds, that is,NetWin(Informed)⁠. In contrast, net win of other funds should not significantly predict abnormal stock returns following contentious votes. Therefore, the predictive power of net win of high vote alpha funds for subsequent abnormal stock returns should be stronger than that of net win of other funds.

3.2.1 Baseline specification

We run the following regression using the panel of contentious proposals to examine the information content of votes cast by the two groups of funds: C A R p = α + b 1 × NetWin ( Informed ) p + b 2 × NetWin ( Uninformed ) p + φ × X p + ε p , (6) where CAR p is the Fama-French-Carhart four-factor adjusted return or the DGTW characteristics benchmark-adjusted return after the vote on contentious proposal p over four different horizons, that is, 3, 6, 12, and 18 months, all starting from the first day after the vote. NetWin ( Informed ) p is the aggregate fractional ownership by high vote alpha funds that win the vote on proposal p minus that by high vote alpha funds that lose the vote. We use strictly ex ante information to construct the net win measure. Specifically, the fractional ownership is based on the most recent quarterly holdings reports of the mutual funds before the proposal is put to a vote, and the vote alpha for each fund is computed using its votes on contentious governance proposals in the 12 months before the vote. We construct NetWin ( Uninformed ) p in a similar fashion for funds other than high vote alpha funds. X p consists of various proposal and firm characteristics, including an indicator for management winning the vote, an indicator for ISS winning the vote, an indicator for management losing a concurrent noncontentious vote, firm size, market-to-book ratio, prior stock return, leverage, capital expenditure, an indicator for S&P 500 firms, mutual fund ownership, and analyst coverage. We also include year fixed effects, proposal type fixed effects, and industry fixed effects in all regressions. We cluster standard errors by firm to allow for within-firm correlation in residuals.

The results, reported inTable 5, show that the coefficient estimate onNetWin(Informed)is positive and significant across all specifications, whereas that onNetWin(Uninformed)is insignificant.23The difference between the two coefficients is significant when we look at abnormal returns during the 3-, 6-, and 12-month windows. These results suggest that high vote alpha funds possess information about the shareholder value implications of contentious governance proposals and that such information is impounded into the stock price over time after the vote. These results also suggest that shareholders can contribute to more efficient corporate decision-making through informed voting.

The economic magnitude of the predictive power of high vote alpha funds’ net win is nontrivial. For example, a one-standard-deviation increase inNetWin(Informed)is associated with an increase of 0.537 and 1.239 percentage points in the Fama-French-Carhart four-factor adjusted CAR over the 3- and 6-month windows after the vote, respectively.24The magnitude of the coefficient onNetWin(Informed)is fairly stable across holding horizons of 6, 12, and 18 months, indicating that the information possessed by high vote alpha funds gets completely incorporated into the stock price within about 6 months and the price stays roughly flat after that. This stability also suggests that the results are driven by permanent changes in stock prices due to information effects rather than by temporary price pressure.

Table 5also shows that proposal and firm characteristics are generally insignificant in predicting abnormal stock returns following the vote on contentious proposals.25These nonresults are expected, given that the proposal and firm characteristics are public information at the time of the vote and we look at stock returns after the vote.

3.2.2 Robustness checks

We perform three robustness checks of the baseline results reported inTable 5.26First, asLevit, Malenko, and Maug (2024)point out, stock price reactions to vote outcomes and shareholder welfare may move in opposite directions when the firm’s stock is illiquid, in which case the shareholder base is more heterogeneous. We thus restrict our sample of contentious governance proposals to those received by firms with more liquid stock, that is, those whose stock ranks in the bottom tercile of Amihud illiquidity ratio. We reconstruct the vote alpha and the net win measures and repeat the regression ofEquation (6)using this restricted sample of proposals. Firms whose stock is in the bottom tercile of Amihud illiquidity ratio account for 56.4% of the contentious proposals. The first two columns ofTable 6report the results using the 6-month CAR as the dependent variable. The results for CARs over other windows are qualitatively similar. Despite a significant reduction in sample size, the coefficient onNetWin(Informed)continues to be positive and significant. Further,F-tests reject the null that the coefficients onNetWin(Informed)andNetWin(Uninformed)are equal at the 1% and 5% levels. These findings mitigate the concern that our results are contaminated by the potential divergence between stock prices and welfare.

Second, we confine contentious governance proposals to those that pass or fail within±10%around the passing threshold. This change affects both the sample of mutual funds and the sample of contentious proposals; it reduces the number of funds that meet the requirement of casting votes on more than 10 contentious governance proposals by about 40% and reduces the number of contentious proposals by about 55%. Columns 3 and 4 ofTable 6show that the results continue to hold with similar magnitudes in this much smaller sample, suggesting that our results are robust to the choice of the cutoff value used to define contentious proposals.

Third, we construct the vote alpha by weighting the votes equally (instead of weighting by dollar holdings). The last two columns ofTable 6show that the results are qualitatively unchanged relative to the baseline results reported inTable 5, suggesting that the results are robust to this alternative weighting scheme.27

3.2.3 Cross-sectional tests

The marginal value of information produced by mutual funds may vary across proposals and firms, which could give rise to cross-sectional heterogeneity in the stock return predictive power of high vote alpha funds’ net win. To shed light on this, we consider four cross-sectional predictions. For each cross-sectional test, we add the cross-sectional variable of interestand its interaction terms withNetWin(Informed)andNetWin(Uninformed)⁠, respectively, toEquation (6).

The first prediction concerns the quality of proxy advisors’ recommendations. A common criticism of proxy advisors is their one-size-fits-all approach to governance (e.g.,Malenko and Shen 2016;Malenko and Malenko 2019). We hypothesize that independent research conducted by mutual funds on a proposal type is likely more valuable if proxy advisors tend to issue blanket recommendations on the proposal type. We consider a proposal type as receiving blanket recommendations if ISS almost always recommends voting for or almost always recommends voting against proposals of that type.28Specifically,I(Blanketrecommendations)is an indicator that equals one if the proposal up for vote belongs to a proposal type on which ISS recommends voting for at least 95% of the time or recommends voting against at least 95% of the time in the past 12 months and zero otherwise. The first two columns ofTable 7show that the coefficient on the interaction term combiningNetWin(Informed)andI(Blanketrecommendations)is positive and significant and that on the interaction term combiningNetWin(Uninformed)andI(Blanketrecommendations)is negative and significant in both columns.29F-tests show that the difference between the coefficients on the two interaction terms is significant at the 1% level in both columns. These results are consistent with the view that information about a proposal produced by mutual funds is more valuable when proxy advisors produce less precise information about the proposal.

The second cross-sectional prediction concerns the ex ante likelihood that the proposal will be contested. The value of having an informed marginal voter on a proposal is likely higher when the proposal is more likely to be contentious. In contrast, information production is likely of little value when the outcome is highly likely to go a certain way. We use the contentiousness of a firm’s past governance proposals to capture the likelihood that the current proposal will be contentious.I(Contestedvotes)is an indicator that equals one if a firm has at least one contentious governance proposal in the past 12 months and zero otherwise. The likelihood of receiving a contentious governance proposal is highly persistent.30Columns 3 and 4 ofTable 7show a positive and significant coefficient on the interaction term combiningNetWin(Informed)andI(Contestedvotes)⁠, an insignificant coefficient on the interaction term betweenNetWin(Uninformed)andI(Contestedvotes)⁠, and a significant difference between the two coefficients.31These results provide evidence that the value of information about a proposal increases with the likelihood that the proposal will be contentious.

The third cross-sectional prediction concerns past firm performance. Poor performance may indicate bad managers or agency problems (e.g.,Morck, Shleifer, and Vishny 1990;Gillan and Starks 2000) and hence may increase the value of information production by shareholders in the voting process. We defineI(Poorperformance)as an indicator that equals one if a firm’s industry-adjusted cumulative stock return in the past 12 months is below –10% and zero otherwise. Columns 5 and 6 ofTable 7show that the coefficient on the interaction term betweenNetWin(Informed)andI(Poorperformance)is positive and significantly larger than that on the interaction term betweenNetWin(Uninformed)andI(Poorperformance)⁠. These results are consistent with the idea that the marginal value of information possessed by informed funds about a firm’s proposals is higher when the firm underperforms.

The last cross-sectional prediction concerns the level of retail ownership. We expect the stock return predictive power of informed voting to become stronger as retail ownership increases because of two reasons. First, compared to institutional investors, retail investors may lack the ability and resources to conduct proxy research. Thus, having an informed marginal voter is particularly valuable when retail ownership is high. Second, retail investors tend to show much lower turnout in voting, in contrast to institutional investors who are generally required to vote. AsBrav, Cain, and Zytnick (2022)show, only 11% of retail investors, who collectively own about one-third of the shares held by retail investors, actually cast their votes. Since a large majority of the proposals use the number of votes as the base to determine the vote outcome, a fund’s votes are more likely to be pivotal when retail ownership is high, which could increase the incentive to produce information. We use an indicator,I(LowIO)⁠, which equals one if a firm’s institutional ownership is in the bottom quintile and zero otherwise, as a proxy for high retail ownership. The last two columns ofTable 7show that the coefficient on the interaction term betweenNetWin(Informed)andI(LowIO)is positive and significant at the 5% level, whereas that on the interaction term betweenNetWin(Uninformed)andI(LowIO)is insignificant. The difference between the two coefficients is significant at the 10% level. These findings lend support to the prediction that high retail ownership increases the value of information production by high vote alpha funds.

3.2.4 Implications for fund performance

Given that high vote alpha funds possess information about the valuation impact of contentious proposals, they may trade on such information. This predicts that high vote alpha funds, relative to other funds, should net buy more (or net sell less) shares after they win a vote than after they lose. We construct a panel of fund-contentious proposal pairs and define a fund’s post-vote net buying of a firm’s stock as the difference in the fund’s fractional ownership in the stock between two reporting dates, 3 months apart, immediately after the vote. We regress a fund’s post-vote net buying on an indicator for the fund winning the vote, an indicator for whether the fund is a high vote alpha fund, an interaction term combining the two, and a set of high dimensional fixed effects.Internet Appendix Table IA-11shows that the coefficient on the interaction term is positive and significant across all four specifications, suggesting that high vote alpha funds exploit their information about the value of contentious proposals. In terms of economic magnitudes, post-vote net buying is 0.168 to 0.211 basis points higher for high vote alpha funds, relative to other funds, when they win the vote, relative to when they lose. Since high vote alpha funds may start to trade right after the vote (as opposed to after the first reporting date), these numbers likely underestimate the actual amount of trading undertaken by high vote alpha funds. Nevertheless, these magnitudes are economically meaningful considering that the net buying variable has a mean of –0.447 basis points and a standard deviation of 6.160 basis points. Combined with the result that it takes about 6 months for the market to fully incorporate the information possessed by high vote alpha funds, this result provides suggestive evidence that high vote alpha funds can potentially benefit from their information production by trading on the information.32

To shed light on the performance of high vote alpha funds, we conduct two tests. First, we examine the performance of trade portfolios of high vote alpha funds.Internet Appendix Table IA-12shows that high vote alpha funds’ stock trades outperform those of other funds, particularly over a longer horizon (ie, 4 to 6 quarters after the trades are made). Since the impact of corporate governance on stock returns is likely long-term in nature, this result suggests that high vote alpha funds, which tend to have a long-term holding horizon, are able to earn abnormal trading profits from their proxy research. Moreover, the outperformance of high vote alpha funds’ trades is disproportionately driven by trades placed around shareholder meetings when proxy research is likely more intense. This result is consistent with the idea that high vote alpha funds use proxy research to inform their trades.

Second, we compare the performance of high vote alpha funds with that of other funds using a calendar-time portfolio approach.Internet Appendix Table IA-13shows that a value-weighted portfolio of high vote alpha funds outperforms that of other funds by 8.9 and 8.1 basis points per month in Fama-French-Carhart four-factor adjusted gross and net returns, respectively. The result for equal-weighted portfolios is statistically insignificant, although in the same direction. These results provide suggestive, though weak, evidence that the investors of high vote alpha funds benefit from these funds’ information production activities in voting.33

3.3 Performance implications of ownership by high vote alpha funds

If high vote alpha funds produce information on governance issues, they might play an effective monitoring role and lead to improved firm performance. Their influence can be in the form of inducing managers to take value-enhancing actions and deterring them from taking value-destroying ones. Besides casting informed votes, high vote alpha funds might actively engage with portfolio companies through, e.g., behind-the-scenes discussions with firm management (e.g.,Carleton, Nelson, and Weisbach 1998;McCahery, Sautner, and Starks 2016) and the threat of exit (e.g.,Parrino, Sias, and Starks 2003;Admati and Pfleiderer 2009). This predicts that, other things equal, ownership by high vote alpha funds should be positively correlated with subsequent firm performance.

We run the following regression using the panel of firm-years to examine the relation between informed ownership and firms’ operating performance: Per f i , t + 1 = α + δ × Informed ownershi p i , t + ν × Per f i , t + ϕ × X i , t + ε i , t + 1 , (7) where Per f i , t + 1 is either ROA or earnings surprises of firm i in year t + 1; Informed ownershi p i , t is the proportion of firm i ’s mutual fund ownership accounted for by high vote alpha funds at the end of year t ; X i , t consists of various firm characteristics measured in year t , including the fraction of governance proposals of which the outcome goes in the direction of management’s recommendation, the fraction of governance proposals of which the outcome goes in the direction of ISS’s recommendation, firm size, market-to-book ratio, prior stock return, leverage, capital expenditure, an indicator for S&P 500 firms, mutual fund ownership, and analyst coverage. We control for lagged performance in all regressions. In some regressions, we additionally include industry × year fixed effects ( Gormley and Matsa 2014 ). We cluster standard errors by industry to allow for arbitrary within-industry correlation in residuals.

Table 8reports the regression results. The coefficient onInformed ownershipis positive and significant across all specifications. In terms of economic magnitudes, the specifications with the full set of controls and fixed effects, that is, columns 3 and 6, show that a one-standard-deviation increase inInformed ownershipis associated with an increase of 0.0023 in ROA and 0.0011 in SUE. These numbers are economically meaningful, representing an increase of 3.0% and 27.2% of the interquartile range, respectively. These results are consistent with high vote alpha funds improving corporate performance by playing the role of informed monitors. The result on earnings surprises also suggests that financial analysts do not fully incorporate the information about high vote alpha funds in their earnings forecasts.

Given that market participants such as financial analysts underestimate the impact of high vote alpha funds on firm performance, informed ownership may positively predict subsequent stock returns. We use a calendar-time portfolio approach to test this prediction. At the end of each quarter from the second quarter of 2004 to the second quarter of 2018, we sort stocks into quintiles based onInformed ownership(the fraction of mutual fund ownership accounted for by high vote alpha funds). Stocks in the bottom quintile have an averageInformed ownershipof 0.81%, whereas those in the top quintile have an average of 53.0%. We use the Fama-French-Carhart four-factor model and DGTW characteristics benchmarks to adjust returns. For the Fama-French-Carhart four-factor model, we obtain the alpha estimates by regressing monthly portfolio excess returns on the monthly returns on the risk factors.

The quintile portfolios are equally weighted in odd-numbered columns and weighted by market capitalization in even-numbered columns. The long-short portfolio that goes long stocks in the top quintile and goes short stocks in the bottom quintile earns an alpha of 39.1 to 56.6 basis points per month in Fama-French-Carhart four-factor adjusted returns and 27.9 to 42.0 basis points per month in DGTW characteristics-adjusted returns. This result indicates that high vote alpha funds can benefit from their information production through an increase in the value of their portfolio companies.

If high vote alpha funds play an effective monitoring role, the positive relation between informed ownership and future stock performance might be disproportionately driven by returns around shareholder meetings when shareholders can influence corporate decision-making through their votes. To test this prediction, we split the monthly returns for each stock into returns in a 3-month period around shareholder meetings (“meeting months”), that is, from 1 month before the month of a shareholder meeting to 1 month after, and returns outside of the 3-month period (“nonmeeting months”). Since firms generally hold one shareholder meeting each year, the meeting months account for a quarter of the months. We compute the alpha of the long-short portfolio for the meeting months and nonmeeting months separately. Panel B ofTable 9shows that the magnitude of the alpha on the long-short portfolio during meeting months is about two times as large as that during nonmeeting months. For example, the DGTW characteristics-adjusted return on the equal-weighted (value-weighted) long-short portfolio is 61.1 (58.5) basis points per month during meeting months, as compared to 36.1 (24.2) basis points per month during nonmeeting months. This result provides suggestive evidence that the monitoring role of high vote alpha funds is a driver of the positive relation between informed ownership and future stock returns.

Combined with the finding that high vote alpha funds tend to have a low turnover ratio, the above results are consistent with the hypothesis that high vote alpha funds, because of their long-term focus, improve firm performance by playing a monitoring role. Nevertheless, we caution that we cannot completely rule out the possibility that the results are driven by high vote alpha funds being better stock pickers with long-lived information.
article.4 Conclusion:
Information production by shareholders is essential for proxy voting to produce efficient outcomes. In this paper, we propose a stock return-based approach to measure informed voting and examine its valuation effects. Our measure, the vote alpha, quantifies the extent to which a shareholder votes in the direction that the market perceives as value increasing. Using a large data set of proxy votes by mutual funds on contentious governance proposals, we find that the vote alpha exhibits moderate persistence, suggesting that it captures a somewhat stable characteristic of mutual funds’ voting behavior. Consistent with the notion that long-term shareholders are more likely to exert monitoring effort, we find that mutual funds with a relatively long holding horizon are associated with a higher vote alpha. We also find that funds that rely less on proxy advisors’ recommendations are more likely to cast informed votes.

Our main finding is that net win of high vote alpha funds (ie, the fractional ownership of high vote alpha funds that win the vote relative to those that lose the vote) positively predicts abnormal stock returns at horizons of up to 18 months following the vote on contentious proposals. This finding suggests that high vote alpha funds possess information about the shareholder value implications of contentious governance proposals and that such information is impounded into the stock price over time after the vote. Cross-sectional tests suggest that the predictive power of net win of high vote alpha funds is stronger for proposals about which proxy advisors produce less precise information, for proposals with a high ex ante likelihood of being contested, among firms with poor past performance, and among firms with high retail ownership. Further, consistent with high vote alpha funds playing a valuable monitoring role, we find evidence that ownership by high vote alpha funds positively predicts firms’ operating and stock performance.

This paper contributes to our understanding of information production in the proxy voting process. A well-functioning shareholder democracy requires that shareholders actively produce information about the proposals up for vote such that their collective choice leads to better outcomes. Our evidence of significant valuation effects associated with informed voting highlights the importance of information production to the efficacy of proxy voting as a corporate governance mechanism. Future research can use our measure to further explore the drivers of informed voting and investigate how best to promote it.
