More broadly, our paper adds to the literature on shareholder activism and the role of institutional investors in corporate governance (see, e.g., Brickley, Lease, and Smith 1988 ; DeAngelo and DeAngelo 1989 ; Carleton, Nelson, and Weisbach 1998 ; Gillan and Starks 2000 ; Huson, Parrino, and Starks 2001 ; Parrino, Sias, and Starks 2003 ; Chen, Harford, and Li 2007 ; Brav et al. 2008 ; Greenwood and Schor 2009 ; Aghion, Van Reenen, and Zingales 2013 ; Appel, Gormley, and Keim 2016 , 2018 ).

4 For example, Iliev and Lowry (2015) consider voting against ISS’s recommendations as a form of active voting. Gantchev and Giannetti (2021) use the tendency to vote independently of ISS recommendations and proposal types as a proxy for mutual fund companies’ active information acquisition. It is worth noting that just like active traders are not necessarily informed traders, active voters are not necessarily informed voters.

5 See Bond, Edmans, and Goldstein (2012) for a review of the literature on information production in financial markets. A number of theories emphasize the role of proxy voting in aggregating investors’ information (e.g., Maug 1999 ; Maug and Rydqvist 2009 ; Bond and Eraslan 2010 ; Levit and Malenko 2011 ; Malenko and Malenko 2019 ; Bubb and Catan 2022 ; Levit, Malenko, and Maug 2024 ).

6 We assume that the benefits of information production outweigh the associated costs at least for some funds, thereby providing them with an incentive to become informed. The benefits include, among others, the potential to influence the vote outcome in the desired direction and to make profits from trading on information gathered from proxy research.

7 This setup closely follows that of Malenko and Malenko (2019) .

8 If a firm has multiple contentious proposals at a given shareholder meeting, we divide the fund’s holdings in the stock of the firm equally across the proposals.

9 Indeed, the median support rate for noncontentious proposals is 97.9%, indicating that these proposals are generally viewed as routine and value-neutral by shareholders.

10 Importantly, the net win measures proposed below at the proposal level are likely to be less subject to noise, because they are constructed by aggregating across many funds that win or lose a contentious vote and taking the difference.

11 Relatedly, the vote alpha measure may be limited in effectively capturing whether proxy advisors make informed voting recommendations. Proxy advisors typically release recommendations well before shareholder meetings, and their recommendations can influence the votes of a large number of shareholders (e.g., Malenko and Shen 2016 ). Consequently, the announcement return around meetings does not fully capture how informed the recommendations are, as the market would have already reacted to the information contained in the recommendations when they are released ( Alexander et al. 2010 ; Ertimur, Ferri, and Oesch 2013 ). Therefore, the vote alpha is likely to substantially underestimate the informativeness of proxy advisors’ recommendations.

12 While some fund families use a family-level corporate governance team to vote proxies, anecdotal evidence suggests that portfolio managers at such families are often actively involved in the process. For example, BlackRock’s Investment Stewardship team is responsible for the voting of proxies at the fund family, yet “[p]ortfolio managers have full discretion to vote the shares in the funds they manage based on their analysis of the economic impact of a particular ballot item” (see http://www.blackrock.com/corporate/literature/publication/blk-shareholder-rights-directiveii-implementation-engagement-policy-2021.pdf ). Also, Vanguard’s Proxy Voting Guidelines state that “issues identified as ‘case-by-case’ in the Guidelines […] are forwarded to the relevant research analyst and/or portfolio manager(s) for their input” and that portfolio managers have “the authority to decide the final vote” (see http://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/313850/000093247119007325/sai046a1020191.htm ).

13 A close vote may also occur due to preference heterogeneity among shareholders, in which case the market would likely react more quickly. Preference heterogeneity in proxy voting can be caused by conflicts of interest (e.g., Davis and Kim 2007 ; Butler and Gurun 2012 ), strategic or portfolio considerations (e.g., Matvos and Ostrovsky 2008 ; Maug and Rydqvist 2009 ; He, Huang, and Zhao 2019 ; Antón et al. 2002), and differential governance preferences ( Bolton et al. 2020 ; Bubb and Catan 2022 ).

14 Our sample funds account for 54.9% of the assets under management by domestic equity funds in the CRSP mutual fund database. Given that larger funds tend to hold more significant stakes in companies and are likely to have more resources for proxy research, this sample selection criteria suggests that funds in our sample are economically important voters.

15 The variable may capture differences in votes across advisors within a fund family when the fund family has both internally and externally managed funds and delegates the proxy voting responsibilities to the respective advisors of the funds.

16 In particular, mutual funds are required to disclose their proxy voting records only once a year; that is, votes cast during the 12-month period ending June 30 of each year have to be filed by August 31 of that year. Also, mutual funds’ quarterly holdings are disclosed with a lag of up to 60 days.

17 We find similar results using more bare-bones specifications without any controls or fixed effects (see columns 1 and 6 of Internet Appendix Table IA-3 ). In untabulated tests, we construct the vote alpha using abnormal stock returns measured over three alternative windows, that is, [ − 1 , + 3 ] , [ − 1 , + 5 ] ⁠ , and [ − 1 , + 15 ] ⁠ . We find that the persistence results remain essentially the same as shown here when we use the CAR over the [ − 1 , + 15 ] window. The results are qualitatively similar, although with reduced statistical significance, when we use the CAR over [ − 1 , + 3 ] and [ − 1 , + 5 ] ⁠ . We conjecture two reasons for the somewhat weak persistence results when using a short window to construct the vote alpha. First, stock price reactions over a short window may not provide the full picture of the value impact of contentious votes, because it might take some time for the market to fully incorporate it. Consistent with this, Cuñat, Giné, and Guadalupe (2012) show that when a shareholder proposal narrowly passes, the abnormal stock return on the day of the vote is 1.3% and over an eight-day window, that is, [ 0 , + 7 ] ⁠ , it increases to 2.4%. Second, informed voters may trade based on their information after the release of vote results, because the vote outcome may only partially reflect the private information held by investors ( Maug and Rydqvist 2009 ; Bouton et al. 2023 ). As such, a long window would be more effective in capturing the information content of contentious votes. Importantly, untabulated tests show that our main results at the proposal level are qualitatively unchanged when we use the CAR over the window of [ − 1 , + 3 ] or [ − 1 , + 5 ] to construct the vote alpha. Thus, while the vote alpha constructed using the CAR over shorter windows appears subject to increased influence of noise, the net win measures at the proposal level are little affected because aggregating across many funds effectively reduces potential noise.

18 Another possible explanation for the moderate persistence in the vote alpha is that mutual funds may vary their proxy research efforts over time, just as they vary their efforts to acquire firm-specific information as in Kacperczyk, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Veldkamp (2014) . Philippon (2006) argues that since managers need to act quickly to seize profit opportunities when product demand is high, shareholders may give management more discretion and subject their decisions to less scrutiny in good times than in bad ones. Indeed, in untabulated results, we find that the vote alpha is significantly higher during recessions than during normal times.

19 Internet Appendix Table IA-3 shows that the tendency to cast a deviating vote from other same-family funds significantly positively predicts both the vote alpha and the likelihood of being a high vote alpha fund.

20 Controlling for fund characteristics in these regressions is important as it helps mitigate the potential for spurious results caused by confounding factors. For example, funds with a small number of stocks and small-cap funds may exhibit more extreme values of the vote alpha. Failing to account for such variables can make it difficult to determine whether the observed persistence is genuine or spurious.

21 We use the Whois data provided by American Registry for Internet Numbers (ARIN) to identify IP addresses registered to fund companies in our voting data set. We match the two data sets by company name and address. We identify the IP address ranges for 304 fund families (ie, institutions) in the ISS voting database that have viewed EDGAR filings between 2003 and 2017. We then use the mapping table provided by Chen et al. (2020) to convert the hidden octets in the IP addresses in the log files to actual octets.

22 To shed further light on the value of proxy research, we compute two vote alphas for each fund family-year with proxy views, one using meetings for which the fund family accesses proxy materials associated with the meetings and the other using meetings for which it does not access such materials. The mean vote alpha using meetings with proxy viewing is 0.273% (with a t -statistic of 3.02), as compared to 0.138% ( t -statistic = 1.89 ⁠ ) for the same set of fund families voting at meetings without proxy viewing. This result provides suggestive evidence that viewing of a company’s proxy materials allows fund families to cast more informed votes on the company’s proposals, although the difference is statistically insignificant ( t -statistic = 1.25 ⁠ ).

23 As alternative specifications, we decompose the net win measures in two ways. First, we construct separate net win measures for funds in the bottom quintile of vote alpha and those in the middle three quintiles. Internet Appendix Table IA-6 shows that neither of these net win measures significantly predicts post-vote abnormal stock returns and their coefficients are insignificantly different. This result suggests that bottom quintile funds do not differ systematically from other uninformed funds in the information content of their votes. Second, we construct an agreement index to measure how much high vote alpha funds agree on a proposal and decompose NetWin ( Informed ) into two parts using the index. Internet Appendix Figure IA-1 shows that when the index is low, it takes about one year for the market to fully incorporate the information possessed by high vote alpha funds. In contrast, when the index is high, it takes about 6 months for the market to fully impound the information. Since it may take more time for the market to fully incorporate the valuation impact of proposals that require investors to process multiple dimensions of information, these patterns provide suggestive evidence that information diversity might be a source of disagreement among high vote alpha funds. See, e.g., Paul (1993) and Goldstein and Yang (2015) for theories featuring information diversity.

24 The magnitude of the results is reasonable given prior findings on the valuation effects of contentious proposals. For example, Cuñat, Giné, and Guadalupe (2012) find that the narrow passage of a shareholder-sponsored governance proposal increases the 1-day abnormal stock return by up to 1.39 percentage points.

25 One exception is the number of analysts, which has a negative and statistically significant coefficient in most specifications. However, the economic magnitude of the coefficient is small. For example, an interquartile increase in the number of analysts is associated with a change of –2.86 and –2.26 basis points, respectively, in Fama-French-Carhart four-factor adjusted returns and DGTW characteristics-adjusted returns over a 6-month window following a contentious vote. Thus, the significant negative coefficient may reflect a risk premium associated with neglected firms that is not captured by our return benchmarks rather than mispricing.

26 Internet Appendix Table IA-5 shows the same set of robustness checks of the fund-level results reported in Table 3 . These additional analyses yield generally consistent findings.

27 We conduct several additional robustness checks and report the results in the Internet Appendix . First, Table IA-7 shows that the results are robust to three additional tests, that is, expanding the sample of contentious governance proposals to include proxy contests, excluding funds consistently following ISS recommendations in the prior 12 months, and excluding shareholder meetings held in May. Second, we construct similar net win measures for active voters and top proxy viewers and include them as additional explanatory variables. Table IA-8 shows that the coefficient on net win of high vote alpha funds continues to be positive and significant, and that on net win of active voters or top proxy viewers is insignificant, suggesting that our vote alpha measure provides a more accurate estimate of informed voting than measures based on deviating from proxy advisors’ recommendations and proxy research. Third, Table IA-9 shows that the results are robust to two alternative clustering scheme for standard errors, that is, two-way clustered standard errors by firm and by year and the wild cluster bootstrap-t method with two-way clustering of standard errors by firm and by year and bootstrap clustering by year ( Cameron, Gelbach, and Miller 2008 ; Roodman et al. 2019 ).

28 We use proposal types defined by the ISS (“ISSAgendaItemID”). For robustness, we use proposal item description (“ItemDesc”) to classify the proposals into finer groups. For example, proposals under ISS Agenda Item ID S0235 are split into two groups, one for proposals that provide shareholders the right to call special meetings and the other for proposals that reduce the ownership threshold for shareholders to call special meetings. We use these finer categories to construct the measure of blanket recommendations by ISS and repeat our analysis. The first two columns of Internet Appendix Table IA-10 show that the results are qualitatively similar to those reported here.

29 Since, as Table 3 indicates, uninformed funds are more likely to rely on ISS’s recommendations, the latter result suggests that institutional investors’ reliance on proxy advisors’ low-quality recommendations can be detrimental to shareholder value.

30 The contentiousness of a firm’s proposals can be driven by relatively stable firm characteristics, such as the degree of agency conflicts and ambiguity in the valuation impact of governance proposals, giving rise to persistence in the contentiousness of proposals. A regression of I ( Contested votes ) on its lagged value using the panel of firm-years yields a highly significant coefficient on the lag of 0.276.

31 The last four columns of Internet Appendix Table IA-10 show that the results continue to hold after adding interaction terms between firm size and the net win measures and those between the number of shareholder proposals and the net win measures. These results alleviate the concern that the contested votes indicator merely captures a firm size effect or shareholder proposal effect.

32 These results echo the findings of Gantchev and Giannetti (2021) and Li, Maug, and Schwartz-Ziv (2022) . Specifically, Gantchev and Giannetti (2021) find that “informed” mutual funds, that is, those that tend to vote independently of ISS recommendations and proposal types, reduce their holdings after the passage of a shareholder-sponsored proposal that they oppose. Li, Maug, and Schwartz-Ziv (2022) show that mutual funds in general are net sellers after losing a vote.

33 One reason for the weak results is that fund managers tend to specialize in strategies where they have a competitive edge, resulting in sorting between managers and strategies. While managers who excel in proxy research may focus on informed voting, those who lack such expertise may opt for other strategies where they have a comparative advantage. Consequently, managers that do not specialize in informed voting may not necessarily experience worse performance than those that do.

34 We focus on the long-short portfolio instead of just the long leg for two reasons. First, the long and short legs provide complementary information about the valuation impact of informed voters. If informed voters play a valuable monitoring role, high informed ownership should be associated with increased firm value. Conversely, the absence of informed voters should lead to the opposite effect. Second, the effect of informed voters deterring value-destroying actions is likely more evident in the short leg than the long leg, because a lack of monitoring in the short leg can lead to more value-destroying actions and negative stock returns.
